title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep17   Flux LoRA Explained! Best Settings & New UI
description:
Welcome to Episode 17 of our ComfyUI tutorial series! In this video, I'll walk you through how to download and use Flux LoRA models to improve your image generation workflow. We’ll cover everything from setting up the models to using the best settings for creating different styles, whether it’s anime, fantasy, or vintage.

Here's what you’ll learn:

- How LoRA models work and why they're great for fine-tuning without retraining the whole model.
- The new ComfyUI menu interface and how to set it up.
- Step-by-step guide on installing and managing LoRA models.
- Tips on using LoRA trigger words for style and object modifications.
- How to combine multiple LoRAs for unique results.
- Speeding up image generation with the Turbo Alpha LoRA.

This tutorial is packed with easy-to-follow tips to help you improve your ComfyUI skills. Don’t forget to leave a like and comment if you found this helpful!

Join our Discord for more tutorials, workflows, and community fun!
https://discord.gg/gggpkVgB
transcript: Welcome to episode 17 of our Comfy UI tutorial series.  Today I want to show you how to download and use Flux Laura models in your workflow  along with the best settings and nodes so you can create even more cool stuff  like these images from detailed anime illustrations to vintage and fantasy themes  including Laura that helps speed up the generation.  Laura stands for Low Rank Adaptation. It's a clever technique used in AI image generation  models like Stable diffusion to make them more versatile without needing to retrain  the whole model from scratch. Imagine you have a huge, fancy recipe book,  the base model that contains recipes for making all kinds of dishes.  Instead of writing a whole new book just for one particular dish or type of cuisine,  Laura lets you create a small note that tells you how to modify the recipes to make, for example,  a special kind of cake, a face, character or style. You don't rewrite the whole book,  you just add a little sticky note with the necessary tweaks to create that new dish.  Since Laura doesn't contain the full recipe, the entire model, it only has those small adjustments.  Therefore, it needs to be applied to the same base model that it was originally trained on.  Using another model is like trying to stick your cake recipe note into a sushi cookbook.  It won't make sense because the base ingredients or model data are different.  You can train a Laura model using a base model and images, but that is more advanced  and I will not discuss it in this episode. Laura's can be used to fine tune many different things.  One type can be a character, Laura. Think of this as teaching the model how to generate a specific  person like a famous celebrity or an anime character. This Laura focuses on making the model  output images of that person with the right features and details. Another type is a style Laura.  This is like putting a filter or artistic style over your generated images. For example,  you could apply a Laura that makes everything look like it was painted in the style of Van Gogh or  like it's part of a comic book. It fine tunes the overall vibe of the image. Then there's the  object Laura. Sometimes you want to add specific objects or concepts to the model's knowledge,  like spaceships, your own product or fantasy weapons. This adds new things. The model can generate  kind of like giving the model a new tool or ingredient to work with. Lastly, there's one more  type of Laura. I'm not sure how to call it, maybe modifier Laura or functional Laura that helps  the model act differently. For example, I will show you a Laura that lets you use fewer steps so you  can generate images faster. Enough with theory. Let's practice. First of all, make sure to go to the  manager and update all. After that, it will ask you to restart. I already updated it earlier.  Now from this episode forward, I will use the new menu interface. Go to settings, then scroll down  until you find the menu. By default, it is disabled, so I will choose to place it on top and now  have this new interface. The manager is where the puzzle icon and button are located. You can move  this area that has the queue button on it. You have some menus on top, like workflow, which lets  you load or save a workflow and edit, which lets you undo or clear the workflow. Let's go to workflow  and open an existing workflow. I will open a flux dev workflow from episode 10. You can get it from  discord if you don't have it already. My favorite flux model is flux dev Q8. If you press Q, it will  start to run this workflow. You can cancel at any time using the cancel button that looks  like an X icon. You can also zoom in and out or use the mouse wheel. You can reset the  view and use pan mode. Once you have a running workflow, you can also turn off link visibility  if it's too distracting for you. I've kind of gotten used to links being visible to see where  each is going. I imagine it as a futuristic interface connected with cables. On the left,  you also have the queue where you can see previously generated images. The node library that lets you  search and drag nodes to the canvas. The models library where you can see all kinds of models you  have downloaded workflows where you can see your workflows and the nodes map from where you can  bypass certain nodes. You can toggle the theme color or you can go to settings and deactivate it  if you don't like it. Okay, let me show you how Laura works. I will make some room here for a new  node. Double click on the canvas, search for load, Laura, and you can use this node to load your  Laura and connect it to the workflow. However, I like to use a custom node that offers way more  options. Search for power, Laura, loader. This is part of the RG3 node pack. You should have this  node if you followed all the tutorials. If not, go to custom nodes in the manager, search for RG3  and install it from there. Now, this node will come just after the node that loads the model.  Remember when we said it's like a sticker on a cookbook. Right now, the model is connected to the  K sampler node and what we want is to insert this Laura into that workflow. So from the node that  loads the model, we connect it to this Laura loader and then we connect it to the K sampler.  Now, it takes into account that sticky note where we tell it what to change. We also have some clip  inputs. So from the dual clip loader, it goes to Laura and from Laura, it goes to both the positive  and negative prompts. So we successfully inserted our Laura into the workflow. You can change it to  another color if you want. Okay, now it's time to get some Laura's. There are different websites  from where you can download them. One is civet AI. If you go to models and filter, you can choose  the model type to be Laura and then the base model. Remember when I told you it needs to be the same  base model. We are using flux dev. So we look for flux D. Here are all the models from that category.  If you click on one, you can see more info like the type, which is Laura, the base model and it's  always important to check to make sure it's the same as your model. Then there are trigger words,  which are also very important. The trigger word is like a special ingredient written on that  sticky note that activates the changes. For instance, the note might say add cinnamon for a spicy  twist. The trigger word here is cinnamon. If you don't mention it, the recipe stays normal.  But once you use the trigger word cinnamon, the dish takes on that specific flavor. From here,  you can download it. Another website is shaker. Here on the right, you have a filter that lets you  select Laura as the model type. Choose the base model, which is flux in our case. If you click on one,  you can see that some of the models don't allow you to use the generated images for commercial work.  But if we go back to our filter, we can select to generate only images that we can use for commercial  work. So in theory, you can use this for commercial work. As you can see here, that doesn't mean you  can generate a Frankenstein monster and sell it. You still need to create images that respect  copyrights and trademarks. In general, if you don't prompt for copyrighted materials, it should be  okay. Just like on Sivit, you can also see the model type, the trigger words, and you can download  the model. Now, for all the downloaded Laura models, you need to place them in one folder,  go to your models folder, then to the Laura folder. Here, you need to place all your models to make  it easier. I recommend creating a folder for each base model. For example, you can create a flux  folder and place all the flux lures there to make sure you don't mix them with SDXL or other  model types. Another website where you can find Laura, but it's a little harder since it's not  as well organized is hugging face. I will give you all the links for the Laura I use in this video.  This Laura is really nice for anime illustrations and is quite popular on Instagram right now.  You can read more about this Laura here. And if you go to the files and versions tab, you can find  the model. Click on the download button and place it in your Laura folder or in the flux subfolder,  like I did here. Always give it a meaningful name or you can name it the same as the trigger words.  If your comfort UI was open, make sure you click on the refresh button so you can actually see the  model in the list. Click on add Laura and you can select one from the list or search for it.  You can see that those in the flux folder are easy to find here since they have flux in front.  There is even an easier way. If you right click on the canvas and go to RG3 node settings,  you have this setting called auto nest sub directory in menu so you can activate it if you have  a lot of Laura's. Right now I don't have over 20 so I will set a smaller number like two so you can  see the effect. Then click save. Now when I click add Laura, you can see those subfolders. I have the  flux folder and you can see all the models inside so it can be quite useful to organize them  this way. Let's add the last Laura we downloaded. Once you add it, it appears here in the list.  You can increase or decrease the strength of this Laura. Just don't go too high. Usually values  of one and under one are preferred depending on the Laura. You can turn it on or off from here.  If it's off, it will not use the Laura. If you right click on it, you can remove it.  Now let's add it again and if you look on the page from where we downloaded it, we have these  words as trigger words. You can copy those and you need to put those trigger words in the prompt.  I usually just put them first at the beginning and then I add my normal prompt afterward.  When you run the workflow, it will load the model and then the extra information in the Laura model.  The first time will be slowed depending on the model size. Then it gets the trigger words and the  prompts and at the end we get this beautiful detailed illustration. Quite cool, isn't it? If you are  wondering how it will look without the Laura active, you can turn it off and this is how it looks  a typical flux image. Let's turn it back on and one cool thing this note has is that if you  right click on the Laura, you have this option to show info. That will show you more information.  You can see here we have trained words. This is it is only one but some have more so you don't  have to remember all the trigger words. You can come here, click on a word and then click copy  that trigger word which you can then paste in the prompt. Alternatively, you can create a note  node and add all your trigger words there. Another interesting lore is this 70 sci-fi style from  Sivit AI. You can see the base model and here is the trigger word. Download it and place it in the same  folder with the rest. You can load it here directly to replace it or remove it and add the other one.  I will click on show info. You can see it has a lot of words here. Which one do you use? Usually  the first ones are more popular so you can select one or more. Since it's on Sivit AI, you can click  on the button that says fetch info from Sivit AI and that will load all the information you need  from Sivit AI. You can see now the first one has a Sivit AI logo so that is the best trigger word  you can use. Click on it, click copy and let's paste it in our positive prompt field. Now we can  add our prompt after that word. If I run it, I get something like this. You can play around with the  lore strength. I like to reduce most of them to 0.8 so it has more creativity while still getting  the lore style. You can reduce it even more to get a mix of both worlds. Another cool lore is this  fantasy lore from shaker. Check the type, the base model and the trigger word. You can see the  recommended setting is 0.8 for weight. Download that model, refresh comfy UI and load it with the  powerlora loader node. Change the strength and show info. It seems this one was available on  Sivit AI as well. Not only on shaker so we can see the best word to use is fantasy but you can  combine it with other words too. I will leave the same prompts and just change the trigger word.  This time the result is different for this lore. We get this fantasy style. Here's another lore  called sketchy pastel anime.  Another lore I found on hugging face is this sticker, Laura. You can find the trigger words here.  From the files and versions you can download it and add it to the same folder with the rest.  Let's load it and add the trigger words in the prompt. The style is different but it doesn't look  like a sticker because I added in the prompt what the background is like in a cave. Let's try one  without the environment. Let me try another simple prompt like a cute cat with a hat. This one  looks like a nice sticker. Now let's try this one called ultra realistic Laura. This one has more  trigger words so you can combine them or use the first one. Here you have some recommended settings.  I'm adding that one fetching the info from Sivit and then copying and pasting the trigger word  in the prompt. Then I add my own prompt. They said to reduce the flux guidance and increase the  steps. I got this realistic looking vintage photo. It's a little blurry but probably the photos  it was trained on weren't so clear. Not sure but it still looks realistic. Let's see how it will  look with an astronaut photo. Yeah it kind of looks real with all those imperfections. Just keep in  mind that some larger Laura can take more time to load. In this case it took almost a minute while  without Laura. It takes me 15 seconds with a different Laura that was smaller. It took 25 seconds.  Keep that in mind. You can see here how big each one is on my drive.  The last Laura is this one called turbo alpha which lets you speed up your generations by  reducing the number of steps needed to only eight steps. This allows you to generate faster  but it does lose a little quality from the files and versions download it and give it a name.  Then all you have to do is load it. It doesn't need any trigger words so you can put your normal  prompt here and I will change the steps to eight. This is the result. The first time took longer  but the second time is faster. As you can see it only took 10 seconds to generate an image with flux  dev Q8. Let's try another prompt. For those who don't have enough VRAM it can be worth a try.  However for me it's worth waiting five seconds more for extra quality. You can also combine  multiple lures. I will load three lures, one for fantasy, one for seventy sci-fi and one for anime  style. I will add all the trigger words in the prompt for each Laura. I set the steps again to 20  or 30 and this is the result. Not bad but for some lures the image can look overcooked because  there are too many things happening at once. I like to split the strength between them.  If I have two I will use 0.5 for one and 0.5 for the other. If I have three I can split it  among all three. As you can see this is the result. It has a mix of all three lures. You can give  more power to the one you want to be more important. If I want the 70s sci-fi style to be more  prominent I add to that and split the difference with the rest. Just keep in mind that using multiple  lures will also slow down the generation. This cute illustration is the result. If I want a more  anime looking image I can change the percentage to give more power to the anime Laura and I get  something like this. Let me load another workflow. These will be available for free on Discord  in the PixelRoma workflows channel. Instead of using Laura with the text to image workflow  let's try using it with an image to image workflow. I will use that anime Laura along with the  trigger word and the prompt. Then I'll load this portrait of a woman. The denoise is set to 0.75  and I get this image that is half photo and half almost like a realistic anime. I can play  with the denoise. If I increase the value the image will be more anime style and less similar to  the original photo as you can see here. Let's try a different Laura maybe the 70s sci-fi style.  I'll adjust the trigger words and the prompt and I got a girl instead because it took the prompt  literally. Let's change girl to woman and try again. We can increase the denoise to see what we  can get and let's increase it even more. Since this Laura works great with illustrations let's  enhance the prompt with some more words related to that like digital painting. You can see how  different prompts changed everything. That's why it's important to play around with denoise and Laura  strength to find the right balance. Right now to make it more similar to my image I need to  reduce the denoise. It looks more similar but I want that illustration style back so then I'll  increase the denoise a little again. Now this is the result I was looking for.
